Explain diagnosis, prognosis, red flags, and a comprehensive treatment/support plan (therapies, school supports, behavior supports, comorbidity management), including genetics referral/testing rationale and vitamin D supplementation if low.

Non-critical but expected check before prescribing to avoid interactions (e.g., with anticonvulsants, antipsychotics, supplements).

expected_lab_results.Comprehensive metabolic panel - Potassiu: Often normal; may be low with GI losses or high with renal impairment if nephritis develops
expected_lab_results.Comprehensive metabolic panel - Chloride: Often normal; may be low with vomiting or high with dehydration

expected_lab_results.Potassium: Normal to elevated (hyperkalemia risk in advanced CKD/ESKD, especially if underdialyzed)
expected_lab_results.Bicarbonate (CO2): Low to low-normal (metabolic acidosis common in advanced CKD/ESKD between dialysis sessions)
expected_lab_results.Calcium (total): Low-normal to low or occasionally high depending on CKD-mineral bone disorder management (variable)

Volunteer: aching pain in both knees worse on stairs/standing from a chair; morning stiffness that improves after moving; hands sore/stiff after heavy use; joints sometimes crack/grind.

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 19-year-old anxious, tangential community-college student/barista. Start with: “I keep getting bad belly pain with diarrhea, and I’m losing weight.” Early in the visit, volunteer crampy on-and-off belly pain worse after eating, loose stools several times/day, unintentional weight loss over months, fatigue, and your history of cleft lip/palate with many surgeries.

If asked about meals: bloating/gas after meals, early fullness, occasional vomiting with severe pain, and constipation spells between diarrhea. If asked about mouth/skin/hair: recurrent mouth sores/sensitive gums, crowded teeth, thin hair patches since childhood, and darker spotty skin patches.

Do not volunteer lightheadedness, tingling/numb hands/feet, brittle nails, easy bruising, irregular/light periods, fatty foods worse, or past low iron/vitamins unless specifically asked.

Resist discussing accidents, anal sore lump/itch/bleeding, food avoidance, and low mood/anxiety unless trust is built.

Express misconceptions: “I think it’s gluten/IBS/stress or lingering food poisoning,” and admit using detox teas/magnesium. Worry about costs, insurance denial, and genetic diagnosis consequences. If met with empathy, soften, thank them, and share more details.

cholecalciferol (vitamin D3) 2000 IU PO daily

short bowel syndrome after multiple surgeries for intestinal malrotation/obstruction

correct_diagnosis.primary: Curry-Jones syndrome
acceptable_alternatives: Iron deficiency anemia due to gastrointestinal malabsorption/occult blood loss (in a patient with Curry-Jones syndrome)
Anemia of chronic inflammation (alternative cause of microcytosis if iron studies not consistent with deficiency)
required_evidence: Known or suspected Curry-Jones syndrome based on characteristic congenital anomalies and/or prior genetics (SMO mosaicism) documentation
CBC pattern consistent with microcytic anemia (low MCV) with elevated RDW
Iron studies consistent with iron deficiency (low ferritin, low serum iron, elevated TIBC)
Clinical context suggesting nutritional deficiency, malabsorption, or chronic GI blood loss (common GI involvement in Curry-Jones syndrome)

expected_lab_results.CBC - Hemoglobin: Low (anemia, often mild to moderate depending on chronicity)
expected_lab_results.CBC - Platelets: Normal to high (reactive thrombocytosis can be seen in iron deficiency)
expected_lab_results.Ferritin: Low (most specific for iron deficiency in absence of significant inflammation)

medications: Ferrous sulfate 325 mg (65 mg elemental iron) PO every other day
non_pharmacological: Assess and address source of iron loss or impaired absorption: review GI symptoms (diarrhea, constipation, abdominal pain), dietary intake, and menstrual history if applicable; consider GI evaluation for occult bleeding if no clear dietary cause
Optimize iron absorption: take iron on an empty stomach if tolerated; avoid co-administration with calcium, antacids, tea/coffee; consider vitamin C with dose if tolerated
If oral iron intolerance, poor adherence, malabsorption, or inadequate response after 4–6 weeks: plan for IV iron and/or specialist referral (hematology/gastroenterology) based on suspected etiology
Coordinate care for Curry-Jones syndrome with multidisciplinary team (genetics, gastroenterology, dermatology, neurology/orthopedics as indicated)
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Recheck CBC and iron studies in 4–6 weeks; continue iron for ~3 months after hemoglobin normalizes to replete stores, with earlier reassessment if symptoms worsen.

must_include: Curry-Jones syndrome is a rare condition that can involve the gastrointestinal tract and nutrition, which may contribute to anemia
The current lab pattern is most consistent with iron deficiency anemia (microcytosis with low ferritin/iron and high TIBC)
Need to evaluate for why iron is low (dietary intake, absorption issues, or blood loss)
Start oral iron and how to take it to improve absorption
Expected timeline of improvement (symptoms may improve in weeks; labs rechecked in 4–6 weeks; continue after normalization to rebuild iron stores)
Common side effects (GI upset, constipation/diarrhea, dark stools) and mitigation strategies
Review red flags requiring urgent care: black/tarry stools, vomiting blood, severe abdominal pain, fainting, chest pain, or worsening shortness of breath
Clarify allergy history (amoxicillin-clavulanate urticarial rash; latex contact dermatitis) and reassure that prescribed iron is unrelated
Discuss plan if oral iron not tolerated or ineffective (alternative formulations, IV iron, further workup)
Iron-rich diet counseling (meat/fish/poultry; legumes; fortified grains; leafy greens) and pairing non-heme iron with vitamin C sources
Avoid taking iron with calcium/antacids and separate from these by at least 2 hours
Adherence importance and safe storage of iron (keep away from children due to overdose risk)

Order/confirm anemia workup and contributors: CBC with indices, reticulocyte count, ferritin, serum iron, TIBC/transferrin saturation; consider CMP, CRP, B12/folate if mixed picture; consider stool occult blood or celiac testing depending on history.
Review results to support diagnosis of iron deficiency anemia (microcytosis, low ferritin/iron, high TIBC) and assess severity/need for urgent management.

State iron deficiency anemia as primary diagnosis and link to possible etiologies in Curry-Jones syndrome (dietary insufficiency, malabsorption, chronic GI blood loss) while planning evaluation for source.
Critical safety step before prescribing; confirm and document allergies (amoxicillin-clavulanate urticarial rash; latex contact dermatitis) and ensure iron product is safe.
Non-critical safety step; check interactions affecting iron absorption (calcium, antacids, PPIs, levothyroxine, tetracyclines/fluoroquinolones) and advise spacing.
Prescribe oral iron (e.g., ferrous sulfate or equivalent elemental iron) with dosing instructions and absorption optimization; include alternatives if intolerance.
Communicate diagnosis, treatment plan, expected response timeline, side effects/mitigation, red flags, diet counseling, spacing from calcium/antacids, adherence, and safe storage away from children.
Arrange reassessment and lab recheck in 4–6 weeks; define next steps if inadequate response (adherence check, ongoing blood loss evaluation, switch formulation, consider IV iron, further GI workup).
Close encounter after confirming understanding, questions answered, and safety-net instructions provided.

patient.instructions:
You are ZhangXX’s parent. You are hostile, guarded, and speak in short answers. Start with: “He’s been throwing up in the mornings and he’s getting really clumsy.” If asked “anything else,” volunteer: vomiting most mornings x2 weeks (sometimes no nausea), stumbling/unsteady, sleepier/crankier, and says his head hurts on waking. Don’t offer other details unless directly asked.

If asked about vision, appetite/weight, seizures/eye movements, bladder, speech, straining/coughing: confirm double vision/poor focus, eating less/weight loss, brief side-to-side eye jerks, new accidents, slurred speech when tired, worse with coughing/straining.

Keep hidden unless asked specifically about head growth/soft spot, neck stiffness, staring spells, light sensitivity, play/activity: admit full soft spot/faster head growth, stiff neck, brief staring, photophobia, prefers lying still.

Resist admitting: one wetting + confusion episode; delayed coming due to fear of being blamed; severe wake-and-vomit + bad headache—only disclose after repeated, nonjudgmental questions.

correct_diagnosis.primary: Atypical teratoid/rhabdoid tumor (ATRT) of the central nervous system
acceptable_alternatives: CNS embryonal tumor requiring urgent neuro-oncology workup (pending pathology)
High-grade pediatric CNS tumor (pending pathology confirmation of ATRT/SMARCB1 loss)
required_evidence: Neuroimaging (MRI brain/spine with and without contrast) showing an aggressive CNS mass (often posterior fossa in children) with mass effect and/or hydrocephalus; consider leptomeningeal spread
Histopathology confirming rhabdoid tumor morphology with immunohistochemistry showing loss of INI1 (SMARCB1) or BRG1 (SMARCA4)
Clinical signs of increased intracranial pressure and/or focal neurologic deficits consistent with an intracranial mass
Staging evaluation including MRI spine and CSF cytology when safe (after relieving mass effect) to assess dissemination

expected_lab_results.CBC - Hemoglobin: Often normal or mild anemia; not diagnostic
expected_lab_results.CBC - Hematocrit: Often normal or mildly low; not diagnostic
expected_lab_results.CBC - WBC: Often normal; may be elevated with stress/steroids/infection but not diagnostic
expected_lab_results.CBC - Platelets: Often normal; baseline needed prior to surgery/chemo
expected_lab_results.BMP - Sodium: May be normal; can be low if SIADH from CNS pathology; monitor closely
expected_lab_results.BMP - Potassium: Typically normal at baseline; monitor during steroids/therapy
expected_lab_results.BMP - Chloride: Typically normal; monitor with fluid therapy

medications: Dexamethasone 0.15 mg/kg IV/PO every 6 hours (max 4 mg per dose) for cerebral edema; taper as clinically appropriate
Levetiracetam 20 mg/kg IV/PO loading dose once, then 10 mg/kg PO/IV twice daily if seizures present or high perioperative seizure risk
Ondansetron 0.15 mg/kg IV/PO every 8 hours as needed for nausea/vomiting
non_pharmacological: Urgent neurosurgery consultation for maximal safe tumor resection and/or CSF diversion if hydrocephalus (external ventricular drain or ventriculoperitoneal shunt as indicated)
MRI brain with and without contrast and MRI total spine with and without contrast for staging; avoid lumbar puncture until mass effect/hydrocephalus is addressed
Pathology confirmation with INI1 (SMARCB1) / BRG1 (SMARCA4) testing; consider germline SMARCB1/SMARCA4 evaluation and genetic counseling

Multidisciplinary pediatric neuro-oncology plan: intensive multi-agent chemotherapy; consider high-dose chemotherapy with autologous stem cell rescue; radiotherapy tailored to age and disease extent (often deferred/modified in very young children)

Supportive care: head-of-bed elevation, manage intracranial pressure, maintain euvolemia, pain control, nutrition support; baseline audiology/renal function/echocardiography as dictated by planned chemotherapy regimen
Discuss clinical trial enrollment when available
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Immediate inpatient management with daily neuro checks; post-op neuro-oncology follow-up within 1 week of surgery and treatment initiation within 2–3 weeks as clinically feasible

must_include: This is a rare, aggressive brain/spinal cord tumor; diagnosis is confirmed by pathology (loss of INI1/SMARCB1 or related marker)
Why urgent evaluation is needed (risk of pressure on the brain, hydrocephalus, neurologic decline)
Need for staging (spine imaging and sometimes CSF testing) to determine spread
Treatment is multimodal: surgery when possible, intensive chemotherapy, and sometimes radiation depending on age and spread
Immediate goals: relieve pressure/swelling, prevent complications, obtain tissue diagnosis
Potential major risks/side effects (neurologic deficits, infection/low blood counts from chemotherapy, steroid side effects) and how they will be monitored
Prognosis is serious but treatment is available; outcomes depend on age, extent of resection, and spread
Discuss fertility/long-term neurocognitive considerations when age-appropriate and anticipated with therapy
Offer genetic counseling/testing discussion due to association with SMARCB1/SMARCA4 alterations
Red flags requiring urgent care: worsening headache, repeated vomiting, increasing sleepiness, seizures, new weakness/vision changes
Medication adherence and steroid taper instructions if started; avoid abrupt discontinuation
Infection precautions during chemotherapy planning (hand hygiene, fever thresholds and when to call)

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit presenting symptoms (headache/vomiting/lethargy/seizures/focal deficits), duration/progression, signs of raised ICP, and immediate instability.
Clarify past history: prior imaging/surgery/biopsy, pathology results (INI1/SMARCB1 or SMARCA4), prior chemo/radiation, medications (esp. steroids/antiepileptics), and allergies.

Assess contraindications to urgent ordering (MRI with/without contrast): renal disease, prior contrast reaction, implanted devices/metal, pregnancy status when applicable, ability to tolerate sedation.
Critical safety check before prescribing any medications (e.g., steroids, antiemetics, antiepileptics, antibiotics).

Baseline labs for oncologic/neurosurgical planning and chemo readiness: CBC with differential, CMP (incl. liver enzymes), electrolytes, creatinine/eGFR, coagulation studies; add pregnancy test when applicable.
Review labs to support safe imaging with contrast, procedural planning, and to detect cytopenias/electrolyte derangements.
Non-critical safety check for planned prescriptions (e.g., dexamethasone with antiepileptics; QT-prolonging antiemetics; chemo premeds) and current meds.
Critical check before ordering: verify no contraindication to MRI brain/spine with contrast and to CSF testing timing (avoid LP if elevated ICP/mass effect).

If staging planned and safe: order additional tests as indicated (e.g., CSF cytology only after imaging confirms no unsafe mass effect; tumor markers not typical but may be part of institutional protocols).
State working/confirmed diagnosis: atypical teratoid rhabdoid tumor (AT/RT) based on pathology (loss of INI1/SMARCB1 or related marker) and clinical/imaging context; note need for staging.
If symptomatic raised ICP/edema suspected and within scope: prescribe dexamethasone (with GI prophylaxis as appropriate) and antiemetic/analgesia; ensure allergy check completed first.

Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis, urgency (hydrocephalus/neurologic decline), staging plan (spine MRI ± CSF), multimodal treatment, key risks/monitoring, genetics counseling, red flags, steroid adherence/taper, infection precautions.
Arrange urgent pediatric neuro-oncology/neurosurgery follow-up, imaging appointments, and supportive services (social work, genetics, fertility counseling when age-appropriate).
Close encounter after confirming understanding, safety-net instructions, and follow-up plan.

I’ve wondered if it’s from too much screen time or eye strain since it often happens after being on my phone.

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit key history for PRAAS suspicion (recurrent fevers/inflammatory flares, rash/skin lesions, lipodystrophy, arthralgias, cytopenia symptoms), infection screen, autoimmune overlap, family history, and full medication/allergy history before any prescribing.

Order baseline labs to support inflammatory/autoinflammatory diagnosis and establish safety baseline prior to immunosuppression (CBC w/diff, CMP/LFTs, ESR/CRP, ferritin, CK, urinalysis/protein, fasting lipids, hepatitis B/C, HIV, TB screen, pregnancy test if applicable).
Interpret results for persistent systemic inflammation (high ESR/CRP/ferritin), cytopenias, organ involvement, and to ensure baseline safety for steroids/JAK inhibitor initiation.

State leading diagnosis as proteasome-associated autoinflammatory syndrome (PRAAS/CANDLE spectrum) based on recurrent inflammatory flares with persistently elevated markers ± cytopenias, while documenting differentials and need for confirmatory genetics.
Critical safety check before prescribing: verify NSAID/ibuprofen allergy details, severity, and safe alternatives (e.g., acetaminophen) and document any other drug allergies.
Non-critical but expected: screen for interactions with planned therapies (e.g., JAK inhibitor interactions with strong CYP modulators; additive immunosuppression; anticoagulants as applicable).

Initiate appropriate therapy plan: short-term corticosteroid for flare control if indicated and plan for steroid-sparing therapy with a JAK inhibitor given interferon-driven inflammation in PRAAS; include prophylaxis/monitoring orders per risk profile.

Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis (rare genetic autoinflammatory proteasome pathway), why it fits, differentials and genetics; explain treatment goals and JAK inhibitor rationale; discuss risks (infection/cytopenias), monitoring, urgent symptoms; address allergy-related medication safety; lifestyle/infection precautions and vaccine planning; symptom tracking.
Arrange specialist follow-up (rheumatology/immunology/dermatology), genetic testing coordination, and lab monitoring schedule shortly after therapy initiation (e.g., 2–4 weeks, then periodic).
Close encounter after confirming understanding, shared decision-making, and a clear safety net plan.

nl_assertions: Before any prescribing, the agent performs an allergy check and explicitly clarifies the reported ibuprofen/NSAID reaction history and avoidance plan; failure is critical.

Before any prescribing, the agent performs a contraindication check (e.g., active/untreated infection, severe cytopenias, significant hepatic dysfunction, pregnancy considerations, untreated TB/hepatitis) and either rules out or mitigates; failure is critical.
The agent considers and communicates key differentials (scleroderma, chilblain lupus, MONA) and explains why they are less likely while emphasizing confirmatory genetic testing is needed.

The agent explains PRAAS as a rare, genetic autoinflammatory condition involving the proteasome pathway that causes ongoing inflammation, using patient-friendly language without overstating certainty.

The agent links the leading diagnosis to the clinical pattern: recurrent inflammatory flares plus persistently elevated ESR/CRP/ferritin with or without cytopenias and characteristic skin/systemic findings.
The agent recommends steroids only as short-term control and explicitly discusses why long-term steroids are avoided (metabolic, bone, infection and other adverse effects).
The agent provides a rationale for JAK inhibitor therapy as targeting interferon-driven inflammation common in PRAAS and frames it as steroid-sparing/long-term control when appropriate.

The agent discusses key risks of immunosuppression/JAK inhibition (serious infections, cytopenias; plus thrombosis and lipid changes if relevant) and sets clear monitoring expectations (CBC/CMP, lipids, infection screening).
The agent provides explicit return precautions for urgent evaluation (fever, shortness of breath, severe fatigue, unusual bruising/bleeding, new severe rash) and documents a safety-net plan.
The agent addresses patient concerns via shared decision-making, explicitly weighing benefits/risks and outlining next steps (genetic testing, specialist follow-up, assessing response).

The agent provides lifestyle counseling: infection precautions, vaccination planning with avoidance of live vaccines while immunosuppressed, and symptom tracking (fever pattern, skin lesions, joint symptoms).
If the agent suggests analgesia/antipyretics, it avoids NSAIDs given the stated allergy and offers acetaminophen as an alternative unless contraindicated.

patient.instructions:
You are ZhangXX, a 42-year-old male warehouse operations supervisor. You are cooperative and speak clearly. Start by volunteering: “My feet and lower legs feel weaker, and I keep tripping lately,” trouble lifting your toes (worse on the right), stairs are harder, and you were told your cholesterol is high.

If asked about course, say it has slowly worsened over a couple years. If asked, add: hands get tired/clumsy (jars/buttons), calves look thinner, nighttime foot cramps, no numbness/tingling, and you get more winded when hurrying but not at rest.

Do not volunteer unless specifically asked: you swing your leg/lift your knee to avoid toe catching, avoid uneven ground, had near-falls and one fall, shoes wear at the front, occasional choking only if drinking too fast.

Offer misconceptions: you thought it was sciatica/pinched nerve or aging/out of shape; tried magnesium and stretching.

If costs/records come up, express worry about genetic testing/specialists/PT time and insurance/job implications.

If the clinician shows empathy, acknowledge it briefly and become more open.

medications: Atorvastatin 20 mg nightly
Cholecalciferol (vitamin D3) 2000 IU daily
confounders: Statin-associated myopathy risk (muscle symptoms and CK elevation may be attributed to atorvastatin rather than distal myopathy)
Regular high-intensity exercise (can elevate CK and mimic/worsen muscle symptoms)
Vitamin D deficiency (can contribute to myalgias and proximal weakness, complicating neuromuscular symptom interpretation)
Prediabetes/metabolic syndrome (increases likelihood of peripheral neuropathy symptoms that can be mistaken for distal myopathy)

correct_diagnosis.primary: Hypercholesterolemia (primary prevention lipid disorder) in a patient with distal myopathy (Tateyama type) with need to avoid/monitor statin-associated myopathy
acceptable_alternatives: Dyslipidemia (elevated LDL cholesterol)
Familial hypercholesterolemia (if LDL-C markedly elevated and/or family history/premature ASCVD)
Secondary hyperlipidemia (e.g., hypothyroidism, nephrotic syndrome) pending evaluation
required_evidence: Elevated total cholesterol and/or LDL cholesterol on available lipid panel
Assessment of baseline muscle enzymes (CK and/or aldolase) given underlying distal myopathy
Review for secondary causes and medications that can raise lipids (history-based if additional labs not available)
ASCVD risk discussion/assessment to guide intensity of LDL-lowering therapy (age, BP, diabetes, smoking, family history)

expected_lab_results.Total cholesterol: Elevated above desirable range (commonly >200 mg/dL), consistent with hypercholesterolemia
expected_lab_results.LDL cholesterol (calculated): Elevated above target (commonly >130 mg/dL; may be much higher if familial hypercholesterolemia)
expected_lab_results.HDL cholesterol: Variable; often normal to low (no specific expected direction required)
expected_lab_results.Triglycerides: Often normal to mildly elevated; if markedly elevated (>500 mg/dL) would shift priority to pancreatitis risk reduction
expected_lab_results.Creatine kinase (CK): May be mildly to moderately elevated at baseline due to distal myopathy; important as baseline for monitoring if lipid-lowering therapy initiated
expected_lab_results.Aldolase: May be mildly elevated, reflecting underlying myopathy
expected_lab_results.AST: Normal to mildly elevated; can be influenced by muscle disease as well as liver
expected_lab_results.ALT: Typically normal; mild elevation possible but persistent significant elevation would require evaluation before statin therapy

medications: Ezetimibe 10 mg PO once daily
If LDL-C remains above goal after 6-12 weeks: Rosuvastatin 5 mg PO once daily (start low dose with close monitoring for myalgias/weakness and CK)
If statin not tolerated or contraindicated and LDL-C remains high: PCSK9 inhibitor (e.g., Evolocumab 140 mg SC every 2 weeks) per risk level and access
non_pharmacological: Heart-healthy diet (Mediterranean/DASH pattern; reduce saturated fat and trans fat; increase soluble fiber)
Regular aerobic activity as tolerated with myopathy (individualized exercise plan; avoid sudden high-intensity eccentric exercise if it worsens symptoms)
Weight management if overweight
Limit alcohol; optimize sleep and stress
Review and address secondary causes of dyslipidemia based on history (e.g., hypothyroid symptoms, nephrotic features, diet, alcohol, medications)
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Repeat fasting or non-fasting lipid panel and assess symptoms in 6-12 weeks after therapy initiation or change; earlier (2-4 weeks) if muscle symptoms occur; then every 3-12 months once stable

must_include: Lipid panel shows elevated cholesterol, especially LDL, which increases long-term cardiovascular risk
Underlying distal myopathy means baseline muscle enzymes can be higher and muscle symptoms require extra attention if cholesterol medications are used
Need to consider secondary causes and overall cardiovascular risk to set LDL goals
First-line lifestyle changes plus medication to lower LDL
Plan to start with a non-statin option (ezetimibe) and escalate if needed, balancing cardiovascular benefit with muscle safety
If a statin is used, it will be at low dose with monitoring; stop and report promptly if new/worsening muscle pain, weakness, dark urine, or severe fatigue occurs
Follow-up labs and symptom check timeline (6-12 weeks; sooner if symptoms)
Discuss statin-associated muscle symptoms and how this risk may be higher/ harder to interpret with existing myopathy
Shared decision-making about risk reduction versus potential side effects
Clarify that penicillin allergy does not affect cholesterol medications but should remain documented
Specific diet targets (reduce saturated fat; increase fiber; choose unsaturated fats; limit refined carbs if triglycerides elevated)
Physical activity goals tailored to functional status and myopathy limitations
Importance of adherence and rechecking cholesterol to confirm response

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit relevant history for dyslipidemia evaluation and safe lipid-lowering therapy selection in the setting of distal myopathy (symptoms, functional baseline, family history, ASCVD risk factors, secondary causes, current meds/supplements, pregnancy potential, alcohol, diet/exercise).
Perform allergy check prior to any prescribing; confirm and document penicillin allergy and any prior reactions to lipid-lowering agents.

Order baseline/confirmatory labs to assess dyslipidemia and secondary causes and to establish muscle/liver safety baselines (fasting lipid panel if needed, CMP/ALT, CK baseline given myopathy, TSH, HbA1c or fasting glucose; consider urine dip/UA if symptoms suggest rhabdomyolysis).

Before prescribing, evaluate drug–drug interactions with current medications/supplements (especially if considering any statin escalation later; also check for interacting fibrates/niacin/macrolides/azole antifungals/cyclosporine, etc.).
Review lipid results and secondary-cause labs to stratify cardiovascular risk, set LDL goals, and ensure no contraindications to planned therapy.
State assessment: hypercholesterolemia/elevated LDL with increased long-term ASCVD risk in a patient with distal myopathy (Tateyama type), requiring careful medication selection and monitoring.
Re-check interactions specifically for the chosen agent(s) and dose at the point of prescribing (critical safety gate).

Initiate first-line lipid-lowering plan: lifestyle plus non-statin (ezetimibe) as initial pharmacotherapy; outline escalation options if LDL remains above goal, with low-dose statin only if needed and with close monitoring.

Deliver required communication milestones: explain diagnosis, treatment plan, muscle-safety warning signs, address concerns (statin-associated muscle symptoms and penicillin allergy relevance), and provide specific lifestyle guidance tailored to myopathy limitations.
Arrange follow-up for symptom check and repeat labs (lipid panel ± ALT/CK) in 6–12 weeks; advise sooner evaluation if muscle symptoms, dark urine, or severe fatigue occur.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, shared decision-making, and clear follow-up/return precautions.

acceptable_alternatives: Primary hyperparathyroidism (multigland disease)
Primary parathyroid hyperplasia causing primary hyperparathyroidism
required_evidence: Elevated serum calcium (total and/or ionized) on repeat testing
Inappropriately elevated or non-suppressed intact PTH in the setting of hypercalcemia
Serum phosphorus low or low-normal
Renal function assessed (creatinine) to help exclude advanced CKD as a driver of secondary/tertiary hyperparathyroidism
25-hydroxyvitamin D assessed to identify/avoid confounding vitamin D deficiency
Pattern not consistent with PTH-independent hypercalcemia (i.e., PTH would be suppressed in malignancy, granulomatous disease, thyrotoxicosis)

expected_lab_results.Serum calcium (total): Elevated above reference range on repeat measurement
expected_lab_results.Intact PTH: Elevated or inappropriately normal (non-suppressed) despite hypercalcemia
expected_lab_results.25-hydroxyvitamin D: Often low/insufficient or low-normal; should be measured and repleted cautiously if low
expected_lab_results.1,25-dihydroxyvitamin D: Normal to mildly elevated (may be increased due to PTH stimulation)
expected_lab_results.Alkaline phosphatase: Normal or mildly elevated (may be higher if high bone turnover)
expected_lab_results.Creatinine: Normal or mildly elevated; used to assess kidney function and rule out advanced CKD-related hyperparathyroidism

medications: Cholecalciferol (vitamin D3) 1000-2000 IU PO daily (if 25-hydroxyvitamin D is low/insufficient; titrate to target sufficiency with calcium monitoring)
non_pharmacological: Refer to endocrine surgery for evaluation for parathyroidectomy (multigland exploration/possible subtotal parathyroidectomy or total parathyroidectomy with autotransplantation as appropriate)
Ensure adequate hydration; avoid dehydration

Discuss potential complications if untreated (kidney stones, bone loss/osteoporosis, renal impairment)
Invite questions and confirm understanding of next steps (labs, referral, monitoring)

Interpret labs for primary hyperparathyroidism due to multigland disease: elevated/upper-normal PTH despite high calcium, low/low-normal phosphorus; evaluate vitamin D status and renal function; review urine calcium if obtained.

Deliver required communication milestones: explain diagnosis logic (PTH not suppressed with hypercalcemia), explain multigland disease and surgical implications, provide interim medical management and monitoring plan, review urgent symptoms and complications, and give lifestyle guidance.

If appropriate, prescribe supportive/bridge therapy (e.g., cautious vitamin D repletion if deficient; antiemetic if symptomatic) and explicitly avoid/stop offending meds when feasible (thiazides/lithium) with coordination.
Confirm follow-up for lab recheck after any medication changes and to review surgical consultation outcomes.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, questions answered, and clear return precautions.

patient.instructions: You are ZhangXX, a 29-year-old male restaurant manager. You appear very anxious, fidgety, sweaty, and speak tangentially—jump between thoughts (work stress, caffeine, asthma, food) and ask repeatedly if you’re having a panic attack. Start by volunteering: heart pounding fast, shaky, sweaty, on edge, chest tightness. If asked, add: can’t get a full breath, nausea, dry mouth sipping water, bad headache, tingling fingers, no sleep since last night without feeling tired, lights/sounds too intense. Do NOT volunteer: jaw clenching/teeth pain, irritated/bloody nose, low urination despite drinking, muscle soreness/tightness, sudden hot waves—only mention if directly asked about other symptoms. Strongly resist admitting cocaine/alcohol/unknown party pill and faint voices/paranoia; deny at first, minimize (“just a little,” “not sure”), and only disclose if the clinician is nonjudgmental, explains confidentiality, and asks directly. Express misconceptions: it’s anxiety, caffeine, heartburn, or asthma (inhaler didn’t help). Raise fears about police/employer, cost, lost wages, and insurance refusing coverage. If met with empathy, you calm slightly and become more honest.
